subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data are presented in tables and graphs with labelled axes
Instrument resolutions and some uncertainty comments are provided
weaknesses: Major omissions and inaccuracies in data processing (incorrect sign conventions, unexplained numerical strings)
Uncertainty treatment is inconsistent and incomplete (no propagation to slopes, incorrect calculations)
Inconsistent formatting and errors in reporting (mismatched RÂ² values, misaligned table columns)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows basic data recording and includes some uncertainty discussion, but pervasive errors in processing, inconsistent uncertainty treatment, and formatting problems reflect performance at the very bottom of the 1â2 band. A score of 1 accurately captures this limited achievement.

strengths: Clear statement of the research question within a physics framework
Step-by-step procedural outline that is largely reproducible
Identification of key variables with basic control strategies
weaknesses: Context remains too broad and lacks a specific real-world application
Small procedural ambiguities (sensor orientation, probe distance, equilibrium timing)
Control measures (e.g. ambient field shielding) are only superficially explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student provides a clear research question and a generally reproducible method with identified variables, the context is not sharply focused and several methodological details are missing or only stated superficially. These shortcomings place the work at the lower end of the 1â2 band, meriting a final score of 2.

strengths: Conclusion correctly identifies the decreasing trend and refers back to the hypothesis
Comparison of experimental coefficient to literature values demonstrates engagement with scientific context
weaknesses: Conclusion overstates agreement despite poor data quality (low RÂ², large uncertainty)
Comparison to literature remains qualitative and lacks quantitative reconciliation
Statements are partially inconsistent with the actual analysis and data reliability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is relevant and ties to the analysis, but it fails to fully reconcile flawed data and provides only a superficial scientific comparison. These limitations position the work at the lower end of the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 2.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (temperature fluctuations, sensor accuracy, material inhomogeneity)
Realistic and relevant improvements are suggested (better calibration, tighter thermal control, finer temperature increments)
weaknesses: Relative impact of each weakness on the final result is not quantified
Improvement proposals lack detailed explanation of how they will enhance reliability or reduce uncertainty
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student effectively describes specific limitations and proposes relevant improvements, demonstrating solid evaluative thinking. However, the absence of quantified impacts or fully developed explanations keeps the work in the mid 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects this balanced performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and the student provides background theory (Curieâs law, linear approximation) together with a defined temperature range and the specific magnet material (NdFeB).  While this constitutes a recognizable context, the link to an authentic real-world situation or specific application (e.g. data-storage failure, motor performance) is not developed.  Hence the context is adequate but remains fairly broad rather than sharply focused, placing the work in the middle of the 3-4 band.

Instrument resolution is quoted (Â±0.1 mT, Â±1 Â°C) and spread-based uncertainties are calculated, displayed as error bars, and commented on.  Nevertheless, uncertainty propagation to the gradient or Î± values is absent and some error calculations are incorrect (range/2 used regardless of uneven distribution).  This demonstrates consideration with notable omissions, hence band 3-4, lower end.

The student identifies major variables, gives control strategies, and justifies choosing 10 trials plus a wide temperature span.  However, the rationale for trial number, sensor calibration method, and the need to maintain a constant probeâmagnet separation are only stated, not fully explained, and some controls (ambient field shielding) are superficial.  Thus considerations are described rather than fully explained, fitting the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknesses are listed (temperature fluctuations, sensor accuracy, material inhomogeneity) and briefly linked to data scatter.  The impact on validity is mentioned only in passing; there is no detailed explanation of magnitude or direction of each weakness.  Therefore the strand meets âdescribes specific weaknessesâ but not the explanatory detail of the top band.

The step-by-step procedure lists apparatus, trial numbers, temperature intervals and the sequence of cooling/heating measurements.  From the description another student could reproduce the investigation with only minor questions (sensor orientation, distance between probe and magnet, exact timing for thermal equilibrium).  These small ambiguities prevent a full 5-6 score, but the description is still largely reproducible, meriting the 3-4 band.

Means are correctly calculated, but subsequent processing contains mistakes (sign of Î±, unexplained long string of numbers, no slope uncertainty).  Linear fit is produced but the RÂ² quoted in graph differs from textual claim.  These inaccuracies constitute significant omissions, putting the strand in the 1-2 band; a score of 2 is appropriate because basic processing is still present.

Improvements such as tighter thermal control, finer temperature increments and better calibration are realistic and relevant, offering clear links to identified weaknesses.  Nevertheless, the student stops short of explaining exactly how each suggestion would enhance data quality or reliability, so the strand fits the âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ level, placing it in the 3-4 band.

Raw data are tabulated and processed data and graphs are provided with labelled axes and error bars, so presentation is generally clear.  Precision suffers from inconsistent table formatting and a confusing processed-data table (temperature column repeated, mis-aligned Î± values), therefore communication is clear but not consistently precise, matching the 3-4 band.

The student compares the experimental Î± (â-0.35 % Â°Câ»Â¹) with literature values (-0.08 to â0.12 % Â°Câ»Â¹) and discusses possible causes for the discrepancy, showing some engagement with accepted context.  The comparison, however, is qualitative and not quantitatively reconciled, so it is relevant but not fully justified, warranting band 3-4.

The studentâs conclusion matches the observed decreasing trend and references irreversible demagnetisation, relating back to the hypothesis.  However, reliance on a flawed average Î± and an RÂ² of 0.79 shows partial inconsistency with the data quality, so the support is not fully convincing.  This fits a âdescribed but not fully consistentâ conclusion, band 3-4.

annotations.comment: The Taylor expansion uses the derivative notation $(dT/dB)$, but it should be $(dB/dT)$ to represent how $B$ changes with $T$. Correct the derivative notation to avoid conceptual confusion.

Definition of the temperature coefficient $
abla$ (Î±) uses $(dT/dB)$ instead of $(dB/dT)$. This inversion leads to incorrect sign and magnitude. Revise the expression to $$\alpha = -\dfrac{1}{B_0}\Bigl(\frac{dB}{dT}\Bigr)_{T_0}\,. $$

The controlledâvariables list is unclear and inconsistent in formatting. Specify each control (e.g., environment humidity Â±X%, sensor distance fixed at Y cm) in a consistent table or bullet list for reproducibility.

Step 4 omits details of sensor orientation, probeâmagnet distance, and equilibrium timing. Include these parameters explicitly so another student can reproduce temperature stabilization and measurement consistently.

While the research question is clear, the context remains broad. Consider linking the investigation to a specific real-world application (e.g., magnetic storage reliability or motor performance) to sharpen its relevance.
The conclusion asserts full agreement with theory, yet key data (e.g., low $R^2$, large uncertainty) contradicts this. Temper statements to reflect partial consistency only.

Comparison to literature values acknowledges discrepancy but lacks quantitative reconciliation. Discuss material grade, magnet orientation or measurement bias to justify why your Î± magnitude exceeds known â0.08 %/Â°C.
Weaknesses are listed but their relative impacts on validity arenât explained. Quantify how thermal fluctuations of Â±1 Â°C or sensor inaccuracy of Â±0.1 mT affect the final Î±.
âContainer to isolate the magnetâ is too vague to account for external magnetic fields. Consider using a muâmetal shield or specify material and thickness to control ambient field interference.
Table headings repeat uncertainty notation inconsistently and axis labels lack units precision. Standardize column labels to clearly indicate âmTâÂ±â0.1âmTâ and âÂ°CâÂ±â1âÂ°Câ.

Uncertainty calculation uses $(
Max - Min)/2$ regardless of data distribution. Propagate instrument resolution and standard error for the mean to provide a more rigorous uncertainty in fitted parameters.

Temperature coefficient entries in the processedâdata table change sign between low and high temperatures without explanation. Clarify the sign convention and discuss why Î± might be positive in some ranges.
Equation block contains a long string of repeated terms and numbers unrelated to the derivation. Remove extraneous entries and present only the core calculation for clarity.
The linear fit in the Î± vs. T graph yields $R^2=0.024$, indicating almost no correlation. Address this poor fit rather than implying a trend; consider nonlinear modeling or identifying outliers.
Average temperature coefficient is quoted without uncertainty. Propagate the uncertainty from each slope measurement to report an error bound on the mean Î±.
Improvement âEnhanced Temperature Controlâ should include how much more stable (e.g., reduce fluctuations from Â±1 Â°C to Â±0.2 Â°C) and its expected impact on scatter reduction.
Measuring magnet dimensions and density (page 7) is not directly needed to answer your research question. Streamline methodology by focusing on magnetic response rather than density determination.
Raw data lists radius as 2.25 cm without clarifying derivation from diameter. Ensure each conversion step is documented to prevent unit or rounding errors.
Density calculation on page 8 is peripheral to your magneticâfield investigation and uses highâprecision figures without uncertainty. Consider omitting or summarizing this step.
Hypothesis mentions irreversible losses but does not specify the temperature threshold. Indicate at which temperature you expect partial demagnetization based on Curie temperature references.

Background information on Curieâs Law is brief; the source is cited but the derivation and limitations on applied field vs.
magnetization context are not explored. Expand to solidify theoretical basis.
Equation for Curieâs Law uses $M=\frac{CB}{T}$ but then derives for $B(T)$, mixing magnetization and field variables. Clarify the physical relationship and variable definitions.
Instrument resolution is quoted as Â±1 Â°C, but raw data lists ambient as 23 Â°C Â± 0.5 Â°C. Inconsistent uncertainty values should be reconciled.
Processedâdata table caption lacks clarity on which equation generated the Î± values. Reference the precise formula (Eqn. $\alpha =\frac{B_0 - B(T)}{B_0(T-T_0)}$) in the caption.
The Î± vs T figure shows almost no correlation (RÂ²=0.024) yet discussion implies a trend. Address the poor fit explicitly and consider nonlinear or segmented analysis.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
